title: Quick check: reading an Incident Report

questions.prompt: Put the steps of reading an Incident Report in the order the lesson teaches.
items: Header — severity, surface, affected entity, customer
Recommendation — what does the SOC want done?
Verify the affected entity (same host or user, same customer)
Summary if the Recommendation surprised you
Evidence and Timeline as you need them — for client comms, replies, documentation
questions.explanation: Header first, then Recommendation, then verify the entity, then Summary as needed, then Evidence as context. Top-down ends in the cardinal mistake.

questions.prompt: Which Incident Report section is the one you ACT ON?

choices.label: Evidence.
choices.rationale: Context, not action. Acting on Evidence is the cardinal mistake.

choices.label: Summary.
choices.rationale: Context. Reading on a need-to basis.

choices.label: Header.
choices.rationale: Metadata, not action.

questions.explanation: The Recommendation is the action the SOC wants taken — the section you act on, full stop.

questions.prompt: The lesson names a "most common new-tech mistake" in reading order. What is it?
choices: Reading the Evidence and forming a disposition before reading the Recommendation.

choices.label: Reading the Header twice to memorise the incident ID.
choices.rationale: Not a named mistake.

choices.label: Skipping the Summary entirely.
choices.rationale: The lesson says you skip Summary and Evidence on the first pass and read them as needed.

choices.label: Replying to the analyst before reading the Evidence.
choices.rationale: Clarifying replies are welcomed; timing isn't the mistake.

questions.explanation: Building a mental case from the raw signal makes the Recommendation feel optional. It isn't.

questions.prompt: The Incident Report Timeline view appears primarily on which surface?

choices.label: EDR only.
choices.rationale: Wrong. The lesson says "ITDR mostly, and a growing subset of EDR."

choices: ITDR Incident Reports, with a growing subset of EDR reports.

choices.label: SIEM only.
choices.rationale: Wrong. SIEM isn't named as the primary Timeline surface.

choices.label: Every Incident Report regardless of surface.
choices.rationale: Wrong. The Timeline is on a subset of reports.

questions.explanation: ITDR mostly; EDR is catching up.

questions.prompt: The lesson warns against one specific misuse of the Timeline. What is it?

choices.label: Using the Timeline to walk a client through the sequence of events.
choices.rationale: Wrong. That's exactly what the Timeline is FOR.

choices.label: Reading the Timeline twice for understanding.
choices.rationale: Re-reading for understanding isn't a misuse.

choices: Using the Timeline to argue with the Recommendation — second-guessing in slow motion.

choices.label: Showing the Timeline to the customer.
choices.rationale: Client comms is a named legitimate use.

questions.explanation: Building a counter-argument from the analyst's chronological view is second-guessing in slow motion.

questions.prompt: An EDR Incident Report lands. Header: High severity, customer Contoso, host SRV-FILE02, analyst Sam P. Summary: encoded PowerShell scheduled task. Evidence: decoded payload. Recommendation: approve the scheduled-task remediation. What do you read FIRST?

choices.label: Summary, top to bottom.
choices.rationale: Wrong reading order. The Recommendation tells you what to do.

choices: Recommendation. Then verify the host. Then the Summary if needed.

choices.label: Evidence — the decoded payload tells you what kind of incident this is.
choices.rationale: The disposition is already made; re-deciding from Evidence is the cardinal mistake.

choices.label: Analyst signature, so you know who to reply to.
choices.rationale: Signature is for the conversation; it's not where you start reading.

questions.explanation: Recommendation, verify entity, Summary as needed.

questions.prompt: Same incident: the decoded payload in Evidence ALSO shows the script tried (and failed) to copy itself to two other hosts. The Recommendation doesn't mention those hosts. What do you do?

choices.label: Approve the recommendation, then isolate the two other hosts on the strength of the Evidence.
choices.rationale: The Recommendation explicitly didn't include those hosts; acting on them is going beyond what the SOC asked for.

choices: Approve the recommendation as written. In parallel, reply to Sam P. asking whether HOST-A and HOST-B should be checked or remediated.

choices.label: Hold off on the recommended action until Sam P. clarifies HOST-A and HOST-B.
choices.rationale: Delaying execution to argue scope on Evidence is the stuck-ticket the SOC model is designed to avoid.

choices.label: Escalate to a senior because the Evidence shows more hosts than the Recommendation lists.
choices.rationale: The right body is the analyst, not the senior. Raise it as a factual reply.

questions.explanation: Execute the Recommendation cleanly; raise the Evidence-side question as a factual reply to the analyst.

questions.prompt: The wrong-tenant rule from the previous lesson has a sibling in this lesson. What is it?
choices: Wrong-host — acting on the affected entity without confirming the right host inside the right customer.

choices.label: Wrong-time — acting before checking the incident timestamp.
choices.rationale: Not named as a parallel mistake.

choices.label: Wrong-severity — acting on a tag without reading the body.
choices.rationale: Different concept; severity-driven response is the severity lesson.

choices.label: Wrong-analyst — replying to the wrong analyst on a multi-analyst case.
choices.rationale: Not a named issue.

questions.explanation: Two customers can have the same hostname; the wrong-host check sits next to the wrong-tenant check.

questions.prompt: A Recommendation reads: "Preserve forensic evidence; do not isolate." What's the right action for a helpdesk tech?
choices: Surface to a senior — preserving forensic evidence is outside the helpdesk ceiling. Don't half-do it.

choices.label: Take a screenshot of the Incident Report and save it to OneDrive.
choices.rationale: A screenshot doesn't constitute forensic preservation.

choices.label: Isolate anyway — better safe than sorry.
choices.rationale: Explicitly overrides the Recommendation. Cardinal mistake.

choices.label: Reply to the analyst asking what "forensic evidence" means.
choices.rationale: Reasonable instinct, but the framing is to escalate to a senior for this action class.

questions.explanation: Forensic preservation is above the helpdesk ceiling; surface to a senior who has the procedure.

questions.prompt: The lesson lists self-checks to confirm the reading-order has landed. Which IS one of them?
choices: Point at the Recommendation section in under five seconds.

choices.label: Recite the analyst's professional background from memory.
choices.rationale: Not in the self-check list.

choices.label: Translate the Recommendation into the customer's preferred language.
choices.rationale: Not in the self-check list.

choices.label: Confirm the customer's billing tier from the Incident Report.
choices.rationale: Not in the self-check list and not something reports surface.

questions.explanation: Speed at locating the Recommendation is the load-bearing self-check.
